summary: Markets are gripped by a risk-off mood as a protracted US-Iran conflict now entering its third month continues to dominate global sentiment, with investors broadly de-risking amid escalating geopolitical uncertainty. Oil prices surged to four-year highs before retreating on volatility around potential US military strikes and Iran's threats over Strait of Hormuz access, while the Bank of England held rates and explicitly flagged Iran war-driven inflation risks, and Brazil's central bank trimmed rates as it monitors conflict spillovers. Energy stocks and jet fuel producers like Repsol are actively repositioning supply chains, consumer goods giant Unilever is signaling incremental price hikes, and gold is climbing as a safe-haven bid strengthens even as Chinese solar manufacturers struggle despite hopes for demand rerouting. The principal risks investors are monitoring include a full Strait of Hormuz closure that would severely tighten global oil and LNG supply, compounding stagflation pressures already building from disrupted rice and petroleum-based commodity markets, alongside transatlantic diplomatic friction as Trump criticizes Germany and reviews troop deployments. In the near term, markets will remain highly reactive to any White House decision on military options against Iran, with oil price volatility, inflation expectations, and central bank policy divergence likely to keep equities under pressure and safe-haven assets elevated.
